request.question: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960

title: Gordon Copley
Regina Zernay Roberts
The Crackerjacks (band)
Michael Stipe
Ivy Jenkins
Mike Davenport
Murmur (album)
No Gods
Joe Raposo (bassist)
Steve Pfauter
sentences: Gordon Copley, born in Pasadena, California, is a bassist who briefly played bass for Black Sabbath.
He played bass on the Black Sabbath song "No Stranger to Love" on their album "Seventh Star".
Bassist Dave Spitz played bass on the rest of the songs on the album.
Copley was also the bassist for Lita Ford's band in early 1985 and joined her on tour after featuring on Seventh Star, leaving Sabbath.
Copley would then later feature on Bill Ward's 1990 album .
Regina Zernay Roberts is an American instrumentalist, singer, and songwriter.
She is lead singer and bass player of Detroit Diesel Power.
She played bass guitar and Moog synthesizer with Scarlet Fever, the all-female backing band that toured with Cee Lo Green during 2010 and 2011.
She toured several years in the New Orleans cowpunk band, Cowboy Mouth; was founding bassist/singer of Los Angeles punk rock band, Méchant; and was bassist for the hard rock band, Halfcocked.
The Crackerjacks were a 1960s Memphis garage rock group.
Band members included organist-bassist Bobby Sowell, lead guitarist David Preola, lead singer Jerry Stamson, and drummer Roy Yeager.
They gained popularity in 1966–67, regularly appearing on Memphis WHBQ TV Talent Party with George Klien (also a popular radio DJ and friend of Elvis Presley).
They won the Mid-South Fair in 1966 for best group.
The Crackerjacks had no bass player; Bobby Sowell played bass and organ at the same time.
The group was short lived due to several reasons.
Sowell was drafted in 1968, Preola also was drafted into the Army, Stampson went into the restaurant business and Yeager went with the group Lobo and the southern rock band Atlanta Rhythm Section.
Sowell was inducted into the Rockabilly Hall of Fame in 2002.
The Crackerjacks are featured in a rock book, "History of Memphis Bands, 1960-72" by Ron Hall, available at bookstores.
John Michael Stipe (born January 4, 1960) is an American singer, songwriter, musician, film producer, music video director, visual artist, and philanthropist.
He is best known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band R.E.M. from their formation in 1980 until their dissolution in 2011.
Ivana Jenkins (née) Vujić (Serbian: Ивана Вујић , born June 15, 1983), known by her stage name Ivy Jenkins is a Serbian-born Canadian bassist.
She played bass for the metal, all-woman band Kittie from October 2007 until March 2012.
She began her career with Kittie by initially filling in for ailing bassist Trish Doan in late 2007, a period which found the band playing concerts in Central and South America.
Before she was in Kittie, Ivy played bass for the melodic hard rock band In The Wake from Toronto, Canada.
In 2008, it was officially announced that Ivy was a full-time member of Kittie and in 2009 she appeared on their fifth studio CD, "In The Black".
Ivy also wrote and recorded bass for Kittie's sixth album, "I've Failed" "You", which was released on August 15, 2011.
Jenkins plays a black Warwick Corvette 5 string bass guitar.
Michael Sean Davenport (born 1968 in Orcutt, California) is an American musician.
Davenport was bass guitarist for The Ataris, joining the band in 1995 prior to the album "Blue Skies, Broken Hearts...Next 12 Exits" in which Davenport co-wrote the song "In Spite of the World".
He also played bass on the album "End Is Forever" and the EP "Look Forward to Failure" on Fat Wreck Chords.
After numerous recordings, tours, and a major label release with The Ataris, "So Long Astoria" in which he co-wrote the song "The Hero Dies in this One" Davenport played bass on "Live at the Metro".
In 2004 Davenport left the band after the Ataris headed in a different musical direction.
In 2005 Davenport appeared in the documentary movie Punks Not Dead www.punksnotdeadthemovie.com "Punk's Not Dead."
Murmur is the debut studio album by the American alternative rock band R.E.M., released on April 12, 1983 by I.R.S. Records.

"Murmur" drew critical acclaim upon its release for its unusual sound, defined by lead singer Michael Stipe's cryptic lyrics, guitarist Peter Buck's jangly guitar style, and bass guitarist Mike Mills' melodic basslines.
No Gods is the debut full-length studio album by British rock band Sharks which was released by Rise Records on 19 March 2012 in the UK and on 20 March 2012 in the US.
The album was recorded in Baltimore, MD, from September to early October 2011 and was produced by Brian McTernan.
The band recorded a total of 14 tracks.
Tony Corrales, formerly of The Exposed, having joined Sharks on a permanent basis, played bass on three songs (James Mattock played bass on all other tracks).
Joe Raposo (born 1970), of Portuguese descent, is the bassist for the seminal California punk rock band Lagwagon and qa engineer lead at Zynga.
He also played bass for a period of time for The Real Mckenzies and Mad Caddies.
Additionally, Raposo plays bass for the San Francisco-based fusion group King City, with fellow Lagwagon member Chris Rest as well as filling in on bass for several live shows with the Dwarves.
Raposo began his career in 1987 at the age of seventeen by joining California hardcore punk band Rich Kids on LSD as their new bassist and remained with RKL until their hiatus in 1996.
He began playing shows with RKL again in 2003 (after they had re-formed a year prior) until their current hiatus after the death of lead singer Jason Sears.
Raposo joined Lagwagon in 2010, replacing original bassist Jesse Buglione.
Raposo also plays bass in the cover band Uke-Hunt.
Steve Pfauter ("Humann") (born 1963, in Francisco, California) is a musician and set dresser and former Bass Guitarist for the Punk rock band The Vandals, from 1980-1984.

Steve was born to German immigrants and attended Los Alamitos High, he joined The Vandals in 1980 and played bass guitar on their debut EP, "Peace thru Vandalism" Steve Human (A.K.A. Human T-Shirt, Humann) designed T-Shirts for T.S.O.L. and also did art work for the Dead Kennedys.
Steve has also played bass in the Original Los Angeles punk rock band Detox, making two full-length albums which featured six songs he wrote while touring the US and Canada.
Later he joined Ken All Night Rocker.
Human also roadied for T.S.O.L. on their first two nationwide tours in 1980-1982.
He was once known for T-shirt designs owning his own silkscreen shop at age sixteen.
He now works in the movie industry as a set dresser and worked on productions such as "Leaving Las Vegas", "High Crimes" and "Spider-Man 3".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960
hop1_docs: Islands (Kajagoogoo album) | Islands is the second album by the British pop band Kajagoogoo, released on 21 May 1984 on the EMI label.  This was the band's first album without lead vocalist Limahl; who had been fired by the band in mid-1983 and went on to pursue a solo career.  Bass player Nick Beggs, already the group's main backing singer, took over lead vocal duties, and also wrote the lyrics.  The album was co-produced by the band themselves, now a four-piece group, along with Colin Thurston, who had also produced their debut, "White Feathers".  It is the final album to-date to feature founding drummer Jez Strode.

Rockaholic | Rockaholic is the eighth studio album by American rock band Warrant, released on May 17, 2011.  The album features the band's third lead singer, Robert Mason, who replaced original lead singer Jani Lane in 2008.  Lane returned to the band for a 2008 reunion tour but by the end of the year, he and the band parted ways for the second time.  Lane had previously replaced former lead singer Jaime St. James, who performed lead vocals on the band's last album, "Born Again", and who initially replaced Lane in 2004.

Fuze (band) | Fuze are an English pop rock band who formed in 2010.  The band consists of Ed Alston (lead vocals and guitar), George Kirchner (lead guitar and vocals), Jack Goldsmith (bass guitar and backing vocals) and Keir Adamson (drums and backing vocals).  They were signed by LUMI Records in 2011 prior to the release of their debut album, "That's What She Said".  The band's name originates from the American drink, Fuze.  Their debut single, Hey You, was used in the soundtrack for the UK film, The Shouting Men, which featured Craig Fairbrass, Warren Llambias, John Barnes and Matt Daniel-Baker.  Their second single, When You Come Home, was released in July 2011.  Fuze were crowned Chelmsford's YFest winners in August 2011 after return from their tour of England.  The band are believed to have finished writing for their second album, which is due for release in 2012.

Andalucia (album) | Andalucia is the fourth studio album by Los Angeles rock band Tito & Tarantula, released in 2002.  The album marked several line-up changes in the band, which had previously consisted of lead singer/rhythm guitarist Tito Larriva, lead guitarist Peter Atanasoff, and drummer Johnny "Vatos" Hernandez.  This album featured the debut of lead guitarist Steven Hufsteter – who had previously played with Larriva in the Cruzados, bassist Io Perry, and keyboardist Marcus Praed.

This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like | "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is the debut single of American industrial singer-songwriter Porcelain Black, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne.  This song serves as the lead single from the singer's debut studio album, "Mannequin Factory".  Produced by Moroccan-Swedish producer RedOne, who had originally discovered Black, "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is influenced by the genre of rock, lyrically stating how the listener should be proud of who they are.  The song was released digitally in the United States on March 29, 2011, and managed to chart at number forty-four on the US "Billboard" Hot Dance Club Songs.

Okie Baroque | Okie Baroque is an EP by American indie rock band Blackpool Lights.  After touring to support their 2006 debut album This Town's Disaster, the band dissolved in 2008 for unknown reasons.  In early 2010 after the reunion of lead singer Jim Suptic's other band The Get Up Kids, it was announced on Twitter that Blackpool Lights had reunited and was recording new material.  The album was released online on November 30, 2010.

Rick Savage | Richard "Rick" Savage (born 2 December 1960) is the bass guitarist and one of the founding members of the English rock band, Def Leppard.  He is called "Sav" to avoid confusion between him and the other Rick in the band, drummer Rick Allen.  Savage and lead singer Joe Elliott are the only remaining original members of the band.  The two of them, plus Allen, are also the only members of the band who have performed on every album.
hop1_titles: This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "Bass player Nick Beggs" and "their debut, 'White Feathers'" appear together (passage [1]). Quote tokens: "Bass player Nick Beggs" (passage [1]); "their debut, 'White Feathers'" (passage [1]).
- A band described as having "dissolved in 2008" (not 2011): "the band dissolved in 2008" (passage [6]).
- Several 2011 mentions but not dissolution: "released on May 17, 2011" (passage [2]); "signed by LUMI Records in 2011" (passage [3]).
- A person with birth year 1960: "Richard 'Rick' Savage (born 2 December 1960)" (passage [7]). Passage [7] identifies him as "the bass guitarist ... of the English rock band, Def Leppard."

2) Ambiguities:
- No passage names a band that "dissolved in 2011." Passages mention dissolution (2008) or 2011 activity but not a 2011 dissolution.
- Multiple bassist names appear (Nick Beggs, Io Perry, Rick Savage) and multiple bands appear (Kajagoogoo, Blackpool Lights, Warrant, Fuze, Tito & Tarantula, Def Leppard). It is unclear which band the question refers to.
- The only explicit "born 1960" token is Rick Savage, but he is described as a bassist, not a lead singer, in the passages. The lead singer born 1960 named in the question is not found here.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entities present in passages: "Nick Beggs", "White Feathers", and "Richard 'Rick' Savage" — none are shown linking a band that dissolved in 2011 to a lead singer born in 1960.
- Missing precise fact needed: the name (page) of the band that dissolved in 2011 and confirmation of which of its lead singer(s) was "born in 1960", plus the debut album's personnel listing showing who played bass on that debut.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
- Priority 1: Fetch a list page of bands disbanded in 2011 (e.g., "2011 disestablishments in music" or "List of bands disbanded in 2011") to identify candidate band(s) that dissolved in 2011. Search keywords: "bands disbanded 2011", "2011 disestablishments in music".
- Priority 2: For any band found that disbanded in 2011, open that band's Wikipedia page and check the lede/infobox and 'History'/'Band members' sections for (a) dissolution year and (b) the lead singer's name and birth date.
- Priority 3: Open the band's debut album page (or the band's discography/debut album section) and check the album's infobox/credits or the "Personnel" section for who played bass on the debut.
- Priority 4 (if needed): If multiple candidate bands match, open each candidate band's lead singer page to confirm "born 1960" (check lede/infobox birth date) and open the debut album page for bass credits.

Final note: I will not answer the question until the specified band page(s), the lead singer's birth date, and the debut-album personnel credits are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: R.E.M. disbanded 2011 Michael Stipe born 1960 Murmur bass player
hop2_docs: Murmur (album) | Murmur is the debut studio album by the American alternative rock band R.E.M., released on April 12, 1983 by I.R.S. Records.  "Murmur" drew critical acclaim upon its release for its unusual sound, defined by lead singer Michael Stipe's cryptic lyrics, guitarist Peter Buck's jangly guitar style, and bass guitarist Mike Mills' melodic basslines.

Michael Stipe | John Michael Stipe (born January 4, 1960) is an American singer, songwriter, musician, film producer, music video director, visual artist, and philanthropist.  He is best known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band R.E.M. from their formation in 1980 until their dissolution in 2011.

Lynda Stipe | Lynda L. Stipe (born September 30, 1962) is an American singer and bass guitarist.  She is best recognized for her involvement in the bands Oh-OK, Hetch Hetchy and Flash to Bang Time.  She is the younger sister of R.E.M.'s lead singer Michael Stipe.
Tanzplagen | Tanzplagen was a rock band formed in Athens Georgia in 1980 by Michael Stipe (vocals farfisa organ), David Pierce (drums), Neil McArthur (Bass) and William Lee Self (Guitar).

Michael Anthony (musician) | Michael Anthony Sobolewski (born June 20, 1954 and legally shortened his name to Michael Anthony in 1978), is an American musician who is currently the bass player in the rock supergroup Chickenfoot and the Circle.  Anthony is best known as the former bass player for the hard rock band Van Halen.
Michael Manring | Michael Manring (born June 27, 1960 in Annapolis) is an American bass guitarist from the San Francisco Bay Area.

Michael Mondesir | Michael Mondesir (born 6 February 1966 in London) is an English jazz bass guitarist, and composer He is one of the most in demand jazz bass players in Europe, touring regularly with Billy Cobham, Cream (band) drummer Ginger Baker and James Brown musical director Pee Wee Ellis.  He is credited as bass player on over twenty major name jazz albums.
response.summary_2: Mike Mills played bass on R.E.M.'s debut album Murmur. Michael Stipe (born January 4, 1960) was R.E.M.'s lead singer until their dissolution in 2011.
